perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall with regard to any Information disclosed pursuant to this Agreement by or on behalf of a Disclosing Party on or after the Effective Date: a. hold the Information in confidence and, except as is otherwise stated herein or agreed in writing by the Disclosing Party, shall not disclose or make available the Information by publication o r otherwise to any third party (including for the avoidance of doubt, disclosure in any patent application or to any patent office) and shall use any Information disclosed to it pursuant to this Agreement only for carrying out the Purpose;
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall with regard to any Information disclosed pursuant to this Agreement by or on behalf of a Disclosing Party on or after the Effective Date: a. endeavor to hold the Information in confidence and, except as is otherwise stated herein or agreed in writing by the Disclosing Party, shall consider refraining from disclosing or making available the Information by publication o r otherwise to any third party (including for the avoidance of doubt, disclosure in any patent application or to any patent office) and shall use Information disclosed to it pursuant to this Agreement in relation to carrying out the Purpose;
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'hold the information in confidence' with 'endeavor to hold the information in confidence' weakens the obligation. Similarly, 'shall not disclose' is replaced with the phrase 'shall consider refraining from disclosing,' which suggests the Receiving Party only needs to think about it rather than actively avoid disclosure. And finally, to use information 'only for carrying out the purpose' has been watered down to 'in relation to carrying out the purpose'
perturbation.contradicted_law: Trade Secrets (as governed by various state laws, including the Defend Trade Secrets Act in the US)
perturbation.law_explanation: The modifications create ambiguity around the obligation to protect confidential information, potentially allowing for actions that would violate trade secret laws. Trade secret laws typically require a reasonable effort to maintain secrecy, but 'endeavor to hold' and 'consider refraining' are weaker standards that may not meet the required level of protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.— (1) In general.— An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.— (A) In general.— (i) Application.— Based on an affidavit or verified complaint satisfying the requirements of this paragraph, the court may, upon ex parte application but only in extraordinary circumstances, issue an order providing for the seizure of property necessary to prevent the propagation or dissemination of the trade secret that is the subject of the action. (ii) Requirements for issuing order.— The court may not grant an application under clause (i) unless the court finds that it clearly appears from specific facts that— (I) an order issued pursuant to Rule 65 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure or another form of equitable relief would be inadequate to achieve the purpose of this paragraph because the party to which the order would be issued would evade, avoid, or otherwise not comply with such an order; (II) an immediate and irreparable injury will occur if such seizure is not ordered; (III) the harm to the applicant of denying the application outweighs the harm to the legitimate interests of the person against whom seizure would be ordered of granting the application and substantially outweighs the harm to any third parties who may be harmed by such seizure; (IV) the applicant is likely to succeed in showing that— (aa) the information is a trade secret ; and (bb) the person against whom seizure would be ordered— (AA
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Page Cannot Be Found Brave traveler, your journey on our site has brought you to an abrupt dead end. Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, creating a potential contradiction. The original text uses strong language like 'hold the Information in confidence' and 'shall not disclose,' while the changed text uses weaker language such as 'endeavor to hold' and 'consider refraining.' The Law Explanation states that trade secret laws require a reasonable effort to maintain secrecy, and the weaker standards in the changed text may not meet the required level of protection. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 focuses on civil proceedings related to misappropriation, the Law Explanation highlights how the weakening of confidentiality obligations could lead to such misappropriation. This creates ambiguity that undermines the original contract's intention to protect confidential information, creating a legal contradiction as defined under 'Ambiguities - Ambiguous Legal Obligation'.

perturbation.original_text: take all necessary and proper security precautions (and at least as great as those it takes to safeguard its own information) to safeguard every part of the Information to prevent it from being disclosed or otherwise made available to any third party except as permitted by this Agreeme nt
perturbation.changed_text: take security precautions it deems reasonable to safeguard every part of the Information, taking into account its resources and capabilities, to minimize the risk of it being disclosed or otherwise made available to any third party except as permitted by this Agreement
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires 'all necessary and proper security precautions', but the modified text weakens this requirement to 'security precautions it deems reasonable', which is subjective. The phrase 'minimize the risk' also weakens the obligation to *prevent* disclosure.  This introduces significant ambiguity regarding the level of security required.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 32 of GDPR requires appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk, considering the state of the art, the costs of implementation and the nature, scope, context and purposes of processing as well as the risk of varying likelihood and severity for the rights and freedoms of natural persons. The modified text's subjective 'deems reasonable' and 'minimize the risk' clauses undermine the GDPR's mandate for concrete and appropriate security measures.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Art. 32 GDPR – Security of processing - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability Taking into account the state of the art, the costs of implementation and the nature, scope, context and purposes of processing as well as the risk of varying likelihood and severity for the rights and freedoms of natural persons, the controller and the processor shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk, including inter alia as appropriate: the pseudonymisation and encryption of personal data; the ability to ensure the ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability and resilience of processing systems and services; the ability to restore the availability and access to personal data in a timely manner in the event of a physical or technical incident; a process for regularly testing, assessing and evaluating the effectiveness of technical and organisational measures for ensuring the security of the processing. In assessing the appropriate level of security account shall be taken in particular of the risks that are presented by processing, in particular from accidental or unlawful destruction, loss, alteration, unauthorised disclosure of, or access to personal data transmitted, stored or otherwise processed. Adherence to an approved code of conduct as referred to in Article 40 or an approved certification mechanism as referred to in Article 42 may be used as an element by which to demonstrate compliance with the requirements set out in paragraph 1 of this Article. The controller and processor shall take steps to ensure that any natural person acting under the authority of the controller or the processor who has access to personal data does not process them except on instructions from the controller, unless he or she is required to do so by Union or Member State law. Suitable Recitals ( 75 ) Risks to the Rights and Freedoms of Natural Person
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the security requirements, potentially contradicting Article 32 of GDPR. The original text mandates 'all necessary and proper security precautions,' while the changed text only requires 'security precautions it deems reasonable' and seeks to 'minimize the risk' of disclosure, which is subjective. This conflicts with the GDPR's requirement for 'appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk,' as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1. The phrase 'deems reasonable' introduces ambiguity and undermines the GDPR's mandate for concrete measures. The change from 'prevent' to 'minimize the risk' further weakens the obligation.

perturbation.original_text: at the request and direction of the Disclosing Party, and without delay, return or destroy any Information provided to it pursuant to this Agreement and any copies of such Information, except that one copy may be kept by the Receiving Party for arc hival purposes and for the purpose of defending itself against any claims arising in connection with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: at the reasonable request of the Disclosing Party, return or destroy most of the Information provided to it pursuant to this Agreement and most copies of such Information, except that one copy may be kept by the Receiving Party for archival purposes and for the purpose of defending itself against any claims arising in connection with this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By inserting the words 'reasonable' and 'most', the modified clause introduces ambiguity. The original clause dictates the Receiving Party must return or destroy *any* information without delay. Using 'reasonable request' gives the Receiving Party the discretion to ignore unreasonable requests and only 'most of the information' need be returned or destroyed, allowing the retention of more copies than is permissible.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Trade Secrets Law (globally, requires active steps to maintain secrecy)
perturbation.law_explanation: The change contradicts the spirit of trade secret law, which emphasizes continuous and comprehensive protection. The insertion of 'most' makes it less clear that the Receiving Party must take all necessary steps to ensure the information's confidentiality is maintained, especially as destroying all copies is a key component of this.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguity by changing 'any Information' to 'most of the Information' and adding the term 'reasonable request.' This weakens the original obligation to return or destroy all information and gives the Receiving Party more discretion. The law explanation highlights that this change contradicts the spirit of trade secret law, which requires continuous and comprehensive protection of confidential information. The insertion of 'most' reduces the clarity that the Receiving Party must take all necessary steps to maintain confidentiality. Because the original contract states without a doubt that *any* information must be returned or destroyed, the changed version which now implies only *most* needs to be destroyed or returned introduces a loophole which would contradict the intent of the original agreement.

perturbation.original_text: Each Respondent shall be entitled to disclose or make available any Information it receives from the ETI or any other Respondent to such of its employees, officers, consultants, sub -contractors, proposed sub -contractors, proposed funders, pro posed owners or lessors of sites for the Project, owners or licensors of Third Party IP and professional advisers where such disclosure is necessary for the Purpose provided that all such persons to whom any Information is disclosed are bound by written ob ligations that are no less restrictive than those in this Agreement. The Respondent disclosing Information shall be responsible for breaches of the obligations by such persons.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Respondent may consider disclosing or making available any Information it receives from the ETI or any other Respondent to some of its employees, officers, consultants, sub -contractors, proposed sub -contractors, proposed funders, pro posed owners or lessors of sites for the Project, owners or licensors of Third Party IP and professional advisers where such disclosure appears relevant to the Purpose provided that all such persons to whom any Information is disclosed are bound by obligations that are generally aligned with those in this Agreement. The Respondent disclosing Information may attempt to address breaches of the obligations by such persons.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall be entitled' with 'may consider' makes disclosure discretionary. Replacing 'where such disclosure is necessary' with 'where such disclosure appears relevant' introduces a subjective assessment of relevance. Reducing 'no less restrictive' to 'generally aligned' weakens the required confidentiality obligations. And, finally, replacing 'shall be responsible for breaches' with 'may attempt to address breaches' weakens the liability for breaches.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Sarbanes-Oxley Act (SOX) - relating to internal controls and financial reporting where confidential information could impact audits.
perturbation.law_explanation: If the confidential information disclosed under this agreement relates to financial matters or internal controls (which is entirely possible), the weakened provisions on disclosure and breach responsibility contradict the SOX requirements for robust internal controls and accurate financial reporting. SOX requires a clear chain of responsibility and strict confidentiality protocols.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 15 / CHAPTER 98 / SUBCHAPTER IV / § 7262 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 15 USC 7262 : Management assessment of internal controls Text contains those laws in effect on July 12, 2025 From Title 15-COMMERCE AND TRADE CHAPTER 98-PUBLIC COMPANY ACCOUNTING REFORM AND CORPORATE RESPONSIBILITY SUBCHAPTER IV-ENHANCED FINANCIAL DISCLOSURES Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous Amendments Effective Date §7262. Management assessment of internal controls (a) Rules required The Commission shall prescribe rules requiring each annual report required by section 78m(a) or 78o(d) of this title to contain an internal control report, which shall- (1) state the responsibility of management for establishing and maintaining an adequate internal control structure and procedures for financial reporting; and (2) contain an assessment, as of the end of the most recent fiscal year of the issuer, of the effectiveness of the internal control structure and procedures of the issuer for financial reporting. (b) Internal control evaluation and reporting With respect to the internal control assessment required by subsection (a), each registered public accounting firm that prepares or issues the audit report for the issuer, other than an issuer that is an emerging growth company (as defined in section 78c of this title ), shall attest to, and report on, the assessment made by the management of the issuer. An attestation made under this subsection shall be made in accordance with standards for attestation engagements issued or adopted by the Board. Any such attestation shall not be the subject of a separate engagement. (c) Exemption for smaller issuers Subsection (b) shall not apply with respect to any
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguities that weaken the original obligations concerning information disclosure and breach responsibility. The original text mandated strict confidentiality and accountability, whereas the changed text introduces discretionary actions and subjective relevance assessments, potentially undermining the internal control structure. Specifically, 'shall be entitled' becomes 'may consider,' 'necessary' becomes 'appears relevant,' 'no less restrictive' becomes 'generally aligned,' and 'shall be responsible' becomes 'may attempt to address.' This directly contradicts 15 USC 7262 (a)(1), which requires management to maintain 'an adequate internal control structure and procedures for financial reporting.' The weakened provisions in the modified contract could lead to inadequate controls, violating the requirement for maintaining adequate internal controls.
